g.text: StatBus Upgrade Timeline (single service; observed-state decides direction — already-at-new continues forward, cannot-reach-new rolls back)
The only way to know these paths actually work is to RUN them — the problem is too hard
to reason out. commit -> push -> CI builds the per-commit image -> run on a real VM ->
observe -> iterate. This diagram is the map (each case + its TEST coverage); the run is
the territory. See doc/install-upgrade-testing.md.
Operator / CI
Operator / CI
systemd (user)
systemd (user)
Upgrade Service
statbus-upgrade@$USER
Upgrade Service
statbus-upgrade@$USER
PostgreSQL
public.upgrade + snapshot
PostgreSQL
public.upgrade + snapshot
operator
operator
Fresh install — seed restore (first boot, before any upgrade)
./sb install (StateFresh) — Seed step:
./sb db seed fetch (pull statbus-seed:<commit_short>) + pg_restore --single-transaction
./sb migrate up — apply only the migrations newer than the seed
Fresh DB only: checkSeedRestored gates the Seed step OFF when the DB holds
user data or applied migrations — a seed restore never overwrites a populated DB.
Seed CREATION is a CI/dev activity — see DEVELOPMENT.md § Database seed.
Service boot
start statbus-upgrade@$USER.service (Type=notify, Restart=always)
advisory lock + LISTEN upgrade channel
(cheap, bounded init)
sd_notify READY=1
(emitted here — heavy recovery below runs in the ACTIVE phase under WatchdogSec)
boot migrate up — schema to the daemon FLOOR (schema-skew guard; STATBUS-145 --to floor)
recoverFromFlag — reconcile a flag left on disk (flag-DRIVEN)
completeInProgressUpgrade — reconcile an in_progress row with NO flag
(flag-INDEPENDENT belt; the SOLE recovery for a pre-flight kill)
Two-layer boot recovery. recoverFromFlag handles a kill AFTER the flag exists (Phase
ladder: old-sb-upgrading / new-sb-swapped / new-sb-upgrading). completeInProgressUpgrade handles a kill BEFORE
the flag exists — the claim -> writeUpgradeFlag window (see § old-sb-upgrading).
It rolls such an orphan to a named terminal state via verifyUpgradeGroundTruth.
TEST COVERAGE — boot
TEST 1-boot-startup-timeout: startup past TimeoutStartSec stays bounded —
heavy work runs post-READY=1 under WatchdogSec, never the static start budget.
TEST 1-boot-advisory-too-early: an advisory-lock attempt before the DB is ready
exits 42 and systemd restarts cleanly — no start-loop wedge.
NO TEST (gap): the belt rolling a flagless in_progress row to terminal is unproven —
see the preflight-kill gap under § old-sb-upgrading.
Schedule + dispatch
./sb upgrade apply-latest →
UPDATE state='scheduled' + NOTIFY
Deploy contract (STATBUS-040/-041): deploy scripts
(standalone.sh / cloud.sh) NEVER pre-stop the service —
systemctl stop is SIGTERM, which an in-flight upgrade
answers with a rollback. ./sb install itself refuses a
progressing upgrade and takes over a crash-looping one
(SIGKILL-class). Binary swaps are atomic renames — no
stop needed for those either.
NOTIFY wakes the listener
claim row WHERE state='scheduled' → in_progress
(started_at set, error=NULL — NO flag on disk yet)
+ reset-failed on own unit (NRestarts counts THIS upgrade only — STATBUS-039 F2)
old-sb-upgrading — backup + binary swap
pre-flight — NO flag on disk yet (row is in_progress, error=NULL)
pre-flight checks — downgrade guard, release assets, disk, signature
Flag-absent window — spans the claim above through pre-flight, until writeUpgradeFlag.
A kill HERE leaves the row in_progress | error=NULL with NO flag: recoverFromFlag finds
nothing to reconcile, so completeInProgressUpgrade (§ Service boot) is the ONLY recovery
— it rolls the row back with a named reason at the next boot.
NO TEST (gap): preflight-kill — a kill in the claim -> writeUpgradeFlag window must converge
the row to a named terminal state via the belt at next boot. Candidate scenario:
killed-by-system-during-preflight-before-flag.
mutex held — Holder="service", flock LOCK_EX on the upgrade-in-progress marker
writeUpgradeFlag — opens the
upgrade-in-progress
marker (
old-sb-upgrading
; stored: "")
maintenance mode on
take
db-snapshot
-> backup_path
git checkout target commit
replaceBinaryOnDisk → stamp
new-sb-swapped
(stored: post_swap)
Fail-fast: any step, before or after the swap, that errors OR hangs aborts the
single attempt and routes to rollback (§ Complete / rollback). No retry.
TEST COVERAGE — old-sb-upgrading (mutex held)
TEST 2-preswap-backup-kill: a kill during the DB snapshot converges the row to a named
terminal — rolled_back, or failed if the restore also fails — never stranded in_progress.
TEST 2-preswap-checkout-kill: a kill during git checkout converges to terminal; the
unswapped binary keeps serving the old version.
TEST 2-preswap-binary-swap-kill: a kill at the replaceBinaryOnDisk pivot is recovered (new-sb-swapped
resume or rollback) — never a half-swapped row that lies about the running version.
new-sb-swapped → new-sb-upgrading — continuing after a crash-restart
exit 42 —
old-sb-swap
: switch to the new sb, request the ONE planned respawn
restart unit on the new binary
fresh boot: cheap init -> READY=1 (watchdog arms)
boot-migrate-up — schema up to the DAEMON FLOOR only (migrate up --to DaemonSchemaFloor)
The new binary BOOTS FRESH — nothing continues in memory; it re-runs the full Service-boot
sequence: cheap init -> READY=1 -> boot-migrate-up -> recoverFromFlag.
STATBUS-145: boot-migrate catches the schema up ONLY to the daemon's operating floor, NOT to
HEAD. The upgrade DELTA is above the floor and runs EXACTLY ONCE at the applyPostSwap migrate
step below — that step is now the real, killable delta site, NOT a no-op. So a death mid-delta
leaves the schema positively Behind → data-safe rollback (the atomicity flip), and boot-migrate
runs only the small, rare FLOOR migrations. boot-migrate runs post-READY with the watchdog armed
and no other WATCHDOG=1 source alive, so it carries its OWN always-ping ticker, bounded by the
shared MigrateUpTimeout (12 h ceiling, STATBUS-095; same exemption as the applyPostSwap migrate).
recoverFromFlag (service.go:682) then READS the upgrade-in-progress marker's phase to learn where the
prior process died. Phase progresses old-sb-upgrading "" (:197) -> new-sb-swapped "post_swap" (stamped
:403, before exit 42) -> new-sb-upgrading (stamped :4339, as resume commits; stored: "resuming").
The SAME dispatch runs on EVERY boot — the planned exit-42 here, or any unplanned-death restart.
alt
new-sb-swapped
marker (stored: post_swap) — the PLANNED exit-42 handoff (the ONE legitimate continue)]
resumePostSwap: if row
PARKED
→ skip (alive-idle, no attempt);
else recovery_attempts++ (attempt START) → resumeEscalation;
3 deaths (budget) / same-step-twice →
PARK
(already-at-new);
else stamp
new-sb-upgrading
(stored: resuming, :4339) + continue
applyPostSwap — remaining steps run on the new binary
config generate + docker compose pull
start DB + wait health + reconnect
./sb migrate up — apply the FULL delta to HEAD (the ONE delta-application site, STATBUS-145);
mid-delta death/failure → observed Behind → data-safe one-shot rollback (atomic)**
start app services + health check
maintenance off
old-sb-upgrading
marker (stored: "") — died BEFORE the binary swap]
recoveryRollback -> roll back (§ roll-back)
restore no-ops: old-sb-upgrading carries an empty backup path
[corrupt/unreadable marker —
discard-and-log
→ normal start [auto]]
[unrecognized phase → [HUMAN:
unknown
new-sb-upgrading
marker (stored: resuming) — died DURING resume:
observed-state
decides]
alt
already-at-new
(observed-state)]
continue-upgrade / complete-upgrade — already at the new version
→ completed
can't-read-position
(db-unreachable / commit-not-fetched) — intermittent error]
→ error classifier (see lifecycle diagram §3)
backoff-retry
: resolves → continue; exhausts → roll back
cannot-reach-new
(binary mismatch / migrations missing, DB reachable)]
recoveryRollback -> one-shot roll back to THIS upgrade's
OWN
db-snapshot
(flag.BackupPath, identity-keyed; flock-serialized)
recoverFromFlag is a phase dispatch: the marker phase decides direction BEFORE anything destructive.
new-sb-swapped (post_swap) → continue forward; old-sb-upgrading ("") → roll back (nothing changed);
corrupt/unreadable marker → discard-and-log, normal start; unrecognized phase → [HUMAN: unknown].
new-sb-upgrading (resuming) → observed-state decides: already-at-new → complete forward;
cannot-reach-new → identity-keyed roll back; can't-read-position (db-unreachable or commit-not-fetched)
→ error classifier (see lifecycle diagram §3): intermittent → backoff-retry, exhausts → roll back.
Full decision tree: see upgrade-lifecycle diagram · doc/upgrade-recovery-model.md.
STATBUS-046 — crash-resume ATTEMPT BUDGET (the per-class routing at the failure chokepoint):
every resumePostSwap entry is one forward ATTEMPT — recovery_attempts++ at attempt START, so a
death self-counts. resumeEscalation bounds the loop that gave rune 10,229 restarts:
• budget = 3 PROCESS DEATHS (deaths = attempts−1) exhausted, OR same-step-twice (deterministic hang, from the flag's dying-step) → TERMINAL
• already-at-new (can't roll back — integrators wrote past maintenance-off) → PARK:
recovery_parked_at + reason; row stays in_progress; service skips resume (alive-idle); siren ONCE
• data-safe / cannot-reach-new → roll back (039 owns direction; 110 makes it byte-safe)
Class A (readiness) waits IN PLACE (size-scaled) and NEVER consumes an attempt; B (deterministic) +
C (resource) → park on first. Un-park = the two deliberate operator triggers only, each a
single fresh attempt with a reset budget.
INVARIANT — kill during migration (re-derive from the diagram):
A kill alone aborts the open transaction -> it re-applies forward on restart -> NEVER a rollback
by itself. Only a committed-but-unrecorded migration (lost-stamp) or an always-erroring migration
makes the re-apply FAIL -> cannot-reach-new -> one-shot rollback.
TEST COVERAGE — phase dispatch + resume after the swap
TEST 3-postswap-resume-died-parked (STATBUS-044, rebuilt 2026-07-04 a SECOND time — supersedes the
deleted 3-postswap-resume-died-rollback, STATBUS-099, and its own 2026-07-03 migrate-up-pinned
build): the two-run resume-re-kill IS product-reachable since STATBUS-046/D3 shipped the
crash-resume death budget (resumeEscalation). STATBUS-099's "product-impossible" finding (VM-run
27820228835: a single kill after container-restart self-healed to completed before a second inject
could land) still holds for THAT injection site (killed-by-system-during-container-restart, AFTER
the applyPostSwap migrate — containersAtFlagTarget can converge there); 3-postswap-container-restart-kill
(single kill, no re-entry) remains the correct coverage for that site. The FIRST rebuild (2026-07-03)
instead pinned both kills at the applyPostSwap StepMigrateUp (3.5) — a 12-run VM campaign (comment
#4/#5) then proved that step is a NO-OP on every real resume: migrations actually apply in the
BOOT-TIME schema catch-up (boot-migrate-up, :1909), which runs BEFORE recoverFromFlag on every
recovery boot, so by the time StepMigrateUp runs the tree is already at the new version — nothing pending. The
SECOND rebuild (this one, comment #6) fabricates the resume state DIRECTLY (in_progress row +
service-held post_swap flag, no dispatch/claim) with a synthetic pg_sleep(3600) migration — the REAL
window, now covered by RecoveryBudgetGuard (commit cc660280f, hoists the attempt count + same-step
stamp to the START of the recovery pass, BEFORE boot-migrate-up). The two kills use DIFFERENT gates
(architect verdict, 2026-07-04, post-hoist review — the flag's step field is never stale-free between
passes): kill #1 gates on flag.step=="boot-migrate" PLUS an active pg_stat_activity pg_sleep(3600)
check (proves the real migration delta already committed, so the kill lands inside the STALL's own
tx, not mid-real-migration); kill #2 gates on flag.prior_death_step=="boot-migrate" instead of
flag.step — step alone would fire on pass 1's residual stamp before pass 2's own guard has even run
(uncounted death), while prior_death_step only flips on pass 2's OWN stamp, atomic with its
already-committed recovery_attempts increment. Two same-step deaths there PARK the upgrade
(alive-idle, siren once via a `.env.config`-only UPGRADE_CALLBACK — STATBUS-131 AC#3 — never
rolled_back) rather than looping loud forever OR rolling back — the pre-046 "one-shot latch => ONE
rollback" framing this note used to carry is ALSO stale: current recoverFromFlag observed-state-gates
the new-sb-upgrading branch (STATBUS-039) — already-at-new always resumes forward via resumePostSwap, never a
blanket rollback-on-any-death.
GREEN: 3-postswap-mid-migration-kill / 3-postswap-mid-tx-kill / 3-postswap-between-migrations-kill /
3-postswap-migrate-killed-after-commit / 3-postswap-migration-deterministic-error (the last two
GREEN since the STATBUS-017 fix landed — see cells (2)/(e)).
The cells of the commit<->record boundary, in execution order:
(1) mid-migration-kill: BEFORE the migration's tx opens (db.migration max unchanged, no schema
change) — forward re-applies cleanly -> completed. GREEN.
(b) mid-tx-kill: kill INSIDE the migration's tx (after BEGIN, before COMMIT) -> Postgres aborts
the uncommitted tx (no committed-but-unrecorded state) -> forward re-applies cleanly ->
completed. GREEN. (3-postswap-mid-tx-kill — the green control proving the boundary's SAFE
side, vs the after-commit cells (2)/(e).)
(2) migrate-killed-after-commit: in the ~ms window AFTER commit, BEFORE the db.migration INSERT
(db.migration max unchanged, but the schema object is already committed). Forward re-apply
FAILS ("relation already exists"), so the ONLY coherent recovery is RESTORE -> rolled_back
(the lost-stamp rollback shape).
BEHAVIOR (STATBUS-017 FIXED, routing updated by STATBUS-039): the schema-skew-guard
`./sb migrate up` runs BEFORE recoverFromFlag on BOTH recovery entrypoints (service boot;
./sb install crash recovery) and re-runs the committed-unrecorded migration -> "relation
already exists" -> migrate up FAILS. With a service-held in-progress flag present the guard
FALLS THROUGH to recoverFromFlag instead of markTerminal+return. Post-039 the new-sb-upgrading
branch consults observed-state: db.migration max < on-disk max with a reachable DB =
cannot-reach-new -> recoveryRollback -> identity-keyed snapshot restore -> rolled_back.
Same terminal as pre-039, now selected by verdict rather than an unconditional latch.
(The refuse path is kept for the no-flag / install-held case. The dead
forward-recovery+restore segment that used to follow the phase branches was DELETED
outright in STATBUS-039 — unknown phases now fail loud.)
3-postswap-migrate-killed-after-commit is the GREEN regression reproducer: it FABRICATES this
state deterministically (committed-but-unrecorded migration object + R1 pinned pre-upgrade
branch + R2 seeded snapshot + R3 tracked migration commit) and asserts rolled_back.
(3) between-migrations-kill: AFTER N is recorded, BEFORE N+1 opens (db.migration max +1) —
resumes the unrecorded pending set N+1..HEAD cleanly -> completed. GREEN.
(e) migration-deterministic-error (3-postswap-migration-deterministic-error): a migration whose
apply errors EVERY time -> migrate up fails the same way as (2); with a service-held flag the
guard FALLS THROUGH to recoverFromFlag -> new-sb-upgrading branch -> observed-state: cannot-reach-new
(migrations missing, DB reachable) -> identity-keyed restore -> rolled_back. Forward is
impossible (errors every apply), so restore is the only escape — the sharpest case for the
fix, and exactly the case observed-state selects the rollback for. GREEN since STATBUS-017.
The commit<->record cells above all fire at the BOOT-MIGRATE step (the pre-recoverFromFlag
schema-skew guard, :1644 / install_upgrade.go:198) — that is where the migrations run on
recovery boots, not at the applyPostSwap migrate.
TEST 3-postswap-migration-timeout (service-dispatch rewrite, 908191f0c): a >WatchdogSec
migration at the boot-migrate after the swap must NOT be watchdog-killed — RED until the
STATBUS-012 always-ping ticker lands, GREEN after (the suite's watchdog/Race-B net; the
pre-rewrite inline version was vacuous — no systemd in its flow).
DEFERRED (not in CI): 3-postswap-worker-ddl-deadlock — a worker-locked migration stays
bounded via pre-DDL quiesce; listed here as intent, not as proven coverage.
TEST 3-postswap-container-restart-kill: a kill between step 11 (start app services) and step 12
(health) is recovered by the next install re-running 11+12 idempotently -> completed.
TEST 3-postswap-watchdog-reconnect: a slow-but-advancing DB reconnect is kept alive by the
deferGating WATCHDOG=1 ticker — systemd does NOT falsely reap it; a true >5-min hang is reaped
by connect()'s own 5-min ctx (via d.reconnect()), not the watchdog.
INVARIANT — lost-stamp window (re-derive from cells (2)/(e) above):
The migration done-stamp is written in a SEPARATE transaction from the migration DDL -> that gap
is the lost-stamp window. A kill in that gap leaves a committed-but-unrecorded schema object;
forward re-apply fails ("relation already exists") -> cannot-reach-new -> one-shot rollback.
Complete / rollback
alt
[all steps succeeded]
mark completed_at (state=completed)
removeUpgradeFlag (mutex released)
supersede older rows + notify UI
Slack callback — OK
TEST 0-happy-upgrade: an injection-free scheduled upgrade runs claim -> old-sb-upgrading -> exit-42 ->
applyPostSwap -> this success branch (state=completed, flag removed) — the baseline the
failure-after-swap scenarios perturb.
cannot-reach-new
(observed-state) — or intermittent exhausted or persistent error (see lifecycle §3)
(already-at-new never reaches here — it completes forward;
can't-read-position → backoff-retry; exhausts → reaches here)]
rollback() — restore git + restore
db-snapshot
(identity-keyed: ONLY this upgrade's recorded snapshot; missing ⇒ [HUMAN: restore-broke],
never another backup; recovery entry is flock-serialized at recoveryRollback)
restart services (docker compose up)
wait for DB health (30s)
SYMMETRIC with applyPostSwap (service.go:3780) — must NOT reconnect before the DB is ready (the scenario-21 race)
reconnect (re-acquire authority lock) + write terminal state (bounded retry)
alt
db-snapshot
restored cleanly AND services healthy]
state=rolled_back (+ error) — healthy at old version [auto]
[[HUMAN:
restore-broke
] — restore itself failed]
state=failed (+ error) — box cannot reach runnable state; needs hands-on
removeUpgradeFlag — ONLY on a landed write
(else KEEP: dead-PID breadcrumb -> StateCrashedUpgrade / belt reconciles)
Slack callback — FAILED siren (fires regardless of the write)
TEST COVERAGE — complete / rollback
TEST 4-rollback-kill: a kill mid-rollback() still converges to a terminal state
on the next recovery pass.
GAP -> FIX (surfaced RED by 2-preswap-backup-kill):
ROOT: rollback() restarted the DB then reconnected with NO DB-health-wait — asymmetric with
applyPostSwap's waitForDBHealth(30s) (service.go:3780) — so the reconnect raced the DB
restart and transiently failed. The terminal UPDATE was then single-shot, silently swallowed,
and the flag removed unconditionally -> row stranded in_progress. The retry alone would only
have papered over the missing wait.
FIX (1) add waitForDBHealth(30s) after restart, before reconnect — the root fix, symmetric with
the normal path (drawn above). (2) the terminal write retries the FULL reconnect (re-acquiring
the upgrade_daemon AUTHORITY lock, service.go:2660 / 1922) + markTerminal on exhaustion +
removeUpgradeFlag ONLY on a landed write (KEEP on failure). On lock-contention, YIELD to the
authority — the belt reconciles; never bypass the lock.
Decision logic: doc/upgrade-recovery-model.md · Names: doc/upgrade-vocabulary.md
Full recovery tree (§1-§4): see upgrade-lifecycle diagram.
